title: Investors on shaky ground as data points to a market slowdown
full_text:
Policy changes in this year's federal budget are likely to have a negative impact on investor demand, and new investor loans, Cotality warns.
The removal of negative gearing for purchases of existing properties is likely to reduce investor lending on a net basis, while some new investors may be drawn to purchase newly constructed properties, though have historically favoured existing dwellings and may now look to non-property assets instead, Cotality's head of research Gerard Burg said.
"With rental yields well below the cost of borrowing, particularly if interest rates rise further, fewer investors into existing properties would be able to generate a positive cash flow, meaning that the housing costs of holding these assets are now substantially higher and serviceability challenges more prominent," Mr Burg said.
Sifting through the March quarter data reveals that Australia's housing demand already took a measurable hit following rate hikes and dropping confidence.
The latest Australian Bureau of Statistics Lending Indicators reflect this shift showing weaker activity than in December last year.
The total number of housing loan commitments fell by 6.2 per cent, while the overall value of lending dropped by 3.8 per cent.
"Owner-occupiers pulled back faster than investors, pushing the investor share loan volumes to a record 41.0 per cent," he said.
"First home buyers showed relative resilience, though their average loan size fell by 2.6 per cent."
Confidence drop
A number of factors have contributed to the slowdown in housing demand.
"Measures of housing affordability and mortgage serviceability were already looking stretched last year, even before the Reserve Bank of Australia started lifting interest rates," he said.
"Consumer confidence surveys also plunged on the back of rising energy prices as the Iran war commenced in late February, with low confidence often acting as a deterrence to a high value purchase, such as real estate.
"All categories of property buyers recorded a quarter-on-quarter decline in lending activity, however owner-occupier lending slowed more significantly than investors.
"The quarterly volume of owner-occupier loans fell by 6.9 per cent, while the volume of investor lending was 5.3 per cent lower."
A similar trend was also evident in value terms, with owner-occupiers down by 4.3 per cent versus a 3.0 per cent fall for investors.
"Within the owner-occupier space, there was a smaller fall in the volume of lending to first home buyers than there was to other owner-occupiers, however this trend was reversed in value terms," he said.
"This suggests that the average new loan size for first home buyers fell in the March quarter, down around 2.6 per cent compared with a 1.6 per cent increase for other owner-occupiers.
"In part, this relative resilience in first home buyer lending may have been supported by the 5 per cent Deposit Scheme."
National slowdown
At the national level, the housing market is already on the cusp of a slowdown, with dwelling values already contracting in Sydney and Melbourne while growth is slowing in the mid-tier capitals, Cotality data shows.
"Demand is likely to soften further as the full impact of the interest rate tightening has not yet been felt," he said.
"There is also the possibility that the RBA could continue to lift rates in the short term given trimmed inflation has remained stubbornly above the central bank's target range."
Buyer behaviour changing
The Real Estate Buyers Agent Association of Australia (REBAA) agrees that tax changes have created two distinct buyer groups - those who rushed to purchase before federal budget night, and those who stepped back entirely in anticipation of the reforms.
"Over the past few weeks, we've seen some buyers wanting to purchase quickly, and others who have paused, waiting for certainty," REBAA president Melinda Jennison said.
She said tax changes would reduce investor participation and place further pressure on already tight rental markets.
"When the rules shift, buyers naturally rethink their strategy - especially those making long-term investment decisions," she said.
"If these changes discourage investment, fewer rental homes will be added to the market, and that will push rents higher.
"Tenants will feel the impact long before the broader market adjusts."
Ms Jennison urged investors to look at the bigger picture, adding that the fundamentals of investing still matter most: location, asset quality, demand, scarcity, cash flow and long-term growth drivers.
"People invest in property for long-term security, wealth creation and financial independence," she said.
"Those motivations don't disappear because of a single policy change.
"They simply require a more considered and strategic approach."
The Federal Capital Press of Australia Pty Ltd
